contraindications: avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless clinician-guided
Use caution with prescription medications and chronic conditions; monitor for intolerance.
safetyNotes: NCCIH: insufficient evidence to know whether oral or topical mugwort is safe; should not be used during pregnancy; breastfeeding safety is unknown.

sources.title: Significance of *Artemisia vulgaris* (Common Mugwort) in the History of Medicine (PMC, 2020)

description: Native to Europe and parts of Asia and Africa; now grows widely including North America; used historically in traditional medicines for GI and gynecological problems; currently promoted for multiple oral uses and in topical combination products. POWO lists native range: temperate Eurasia to Indo-China and North Africa.

region: Native range: temperate Eurasia to Indo-China and North Africa (POWO); NCCIH describes native Europe and parts of Asia/Africa with global spread.
therapeuticUses: historical use for symptom relief in regional folk medicine
traditionalUse: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine
sideEffects: mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or loose stool)
allergic rash or itching in sensitive individuals
summary: NCCIH states very little human research exists; there is not enough evidence to determine whether mugwort is safe or useful for any health condition.
